items.name: Dice set
items.description: This item encompasses a wide range of game pieces, including dice and decks of cards (for games such as Three-Dragon Ante). A few common examples appear on the Tools table, but other kinds of gaming sets exist. If you are proficient with a gaming set, you can add your proficiency bonus to ability checks you make to play a game with that set. Each type of gaming set requires a separate proficiency.

items.name: Pouch
items.description: A cloth or leather pouch can hold up to 20 sling bullets or 50 blowgun needles, among other things. A compartmentalized pouch for holding spell components is called a component pouch (described earlier in this section).

traits: I'm full of inspiring and cautionary tales from my military experience relevant to almost every combat situation.
I'm haunted by memories of war. I can't get the images of violence out of my mind.
I've lost too many friends, and I'm slow to make new ones.
I can stare down a hell hound without flinching.
I enjoy being strong and like breaking things.
I have a crude sense of humor.
I face problems head-on. A simple, direct solution is the best path to success.
ideals: Greater Good. Our lot is to lay down our lives in defense of others. (Good)
Responsibility. I do what must be done and follow orders, even if I don't like them. (Lawful)
Independence. When people follow orders blindly, they embrace a kind of tyranny. (Chaotic)
Might. In life as in war, the stronger force prevails. (Evil)
Live and Let Live. Ideals aren't worth killing over or going to war for. (Neutral)
Nation. My city, nation, or people are all that matter. (Any)
bonds: I would still lay down my life for the people I served with.
Someone saved my life on the battlefield. To this day, I will never leave a friend behind.
I'll never forget the crushing defeat my company suffered or the enemies who dealt it.
Those who fight beside me are those worth dying for.
I fight for those who cannot fight for themselves.
flaws: The monstrous enemy we faced in battle still leaves me quivering with fear.
I have little respect for anyone who is not a proven warrior.
I made a terrible mistake in battle that cost many lives, and I would do anything to keep that mistake secret.
My hatred of my enemies is blind and unreasoning.
I obey the law, even if the law causes misery.
I'd rather eat my armor than admit when I'm wrong.

activeQuests.title: The First Step
activeQuests.description: You awake in a strange clearing. The air is fresh, but the silence is heavy. You must get your bearings.

codexEntries.title: Goblin: The Cunning Cinder‑Kin of the Wilds
codexEntries.content:
**Physical Description**
Goblins stand between three and four feet tall, their wiry frames covered in coarse, mottled skin that ranges from dusky green to ashen gray. Their heads are proportionally large, bearing pointed ears, narrow, beady eyes, and a snout tipped with a set of sharp, constantly gnashing teeth. A sparse mane of wiry hair crowns their skulls, often dyed with the ochre of dried blood or the ash of their latest hearth. Their limbs are long for their size, ending in claw‑like fingers that excel at both digging and pilfering.

**Biology**
While the exact lineage of goblins remains debated, recent studies of their hemoglobin suggest a close kinship to the lesser draconic reptiles of the southern swamps. Their metabolism is unusually fast, allowing them to subsist on a diet of carrion, insects, and the occasional stolen morsel from larger beasts. Their digestive tracts possess a dual‑chambered stomach, one acidic chamber for breaking down tough protein, and a second fermentative chamber that extracts nutrients from fibrous plant matter—a trait that explains their occasional forays into mushroom farms and root gardens.

**Ecology**
Goblins are opportunistic survivors. They carve out warrens in limestone caves, abandoned mine shafts, and the hollowed roots of ancient trees. These dens are interconnected by a network of tunnels that double as trap‑laced ambush points. Goblins are rarely solitary; they organize into loose bands of five to thirty, led by the strongest or the most cunning—often a “chief” who bears a jagged iron crown fashioned from scavenged armor. Their societies are fluid, with members constantly shifting allegiances based on the promise of loot or safety. They maintain a symbiotic relationship with certain fungi, cultivating spore‑filled chambers that both provide nourishment and emit a faint, intoxicating haze that dulls the senses of intruders.

**Myths & Legends**
Legends speak of the “Goblin King’s Crown,” a relic said to grant its wearer mastery over all goblin kind. Some tales claim that goblins once served as covert agents for forgotten deities, whispering secrets into the ears of mortals. In the highlands, wandering bards recount the “Red‑Eye Prophecy,” a vision wherein a legion of goblins, eyes glowing like ember, will rise to claim the moonlit valleys. While most scholars dismiss these stories as folklore, the persistent appearance of unusually coordinated goblin raids during eclipses fuels speculation that some deeper, perhaps magical, influence guides their actions.

**Survivor’s Tale**
"I thought the darkness was my end, until the goblin chief’s laugh cut through the night like a blade. I ran, heart pounding, and slipped past a tunnel riddled with glowing fungi. When I emerged, the sunrise painted the forest gold, and the goblins were already nursing their wounds, their eyes dimmed but still watching. I left my sword behind—those creatures respect strength, even in the faintest ember of a human soul."
—Eldric Thorne, wandering swordsman, recounting his escape from the Maw of Blackrock Hollow

codexEntries.title: The Nullling, Shadow of Unreality
codexEntries.content:
**Physical Description**
The Nullling manifests as a wavering silhouette of darkness, standing roughly two and a half meters tall. Its "skin" is a liquid‑like membrane of void‑black, perpetually rippling as though caught in a slow tide of night. Within the membrane flicker faint pinpricks that resemble distant stars, giving the creature an ever‑shifting constellation across its form. It possesses no discernible facial features; instead, a faint auroral halo crowns its head, pulsing in a cadence that mirrors the thoughts of nearby sentient beings. Its limbs are elongated and joint‑less, ending in tapered tips that seem to dissolve into mist when they touch the ground.

**Biology**
Scholars of the Arcanum suggest the Nullling is a manifestation of raw negentropy, a living paradox that feeds on the ambient magical potential of its surroundings. Its "cells" are not cellular at all, but clusters of quivering probability vectors that can rearrange themselves to alter the creature's shape. When it consumes magical energy—whether from ley lines, spellcasters, or enchanted artifacts—it temporarily solidifies, gaining greater mass and a more defined visage. Conversely, in areas barren of arcane flux, the Nullling becomes almost ethereal, able to drift through cracks and even slip between the planes for brief moments.

**Ecology**
The Nullling is most commonly reported in the forgotten ruins of the Fallen Empire, where ley‑line intersections have long since been abandoned. It prefers locations where the veil between the Material Plane and the Shadowfell thins, such as deep caverns, moon‑lit glades, and the peripheries of cursed battlefields. Though solitary by nature, several Nulllings have been observed gathering around a single source of potent magical overflow—often a crystal heart or a lingering dragon’s hoard—forming a temporary chorus of silence that can suppress all sound within a hundred‑meter radius.

**Myths and Legends**
Tales among the nomadic Starlit Tribes speak of the "Silent Maw," a devourer that swallows not flesh but memory. It is said that those who stare into its auroral crown see the last moments of their own lives, and that the creature can erase a person’s name from the world’s record, leaving no trace of their deeds. Some bards claim the Nullling is the unborn child of the primordial darkness that birthed the void, sent to cleanse the realms of over‑abundant magic. Others whisper that it is a guardian, an accidental by‑product of the world’s creation, tasked with maintaining the balance between creation and oblivion.

**Survivor’s Tale**
"I thought the darkness itself had taken me, but when the Nullling vanished, the silence was louder than any scream. My companions fell one by one, each as if their very thoughts were snuffed out. I clutched the crystal amulet of my grandmother, and the creature recoiled, its aurora dimming like a dying star. I ran, heart pounding, until the first light of dawn pierced the cavern walls. Even now, the memory of that cold, weightless void haunts my dreams, and I hear its pulse whenever I near a place where magic sleeps."
